name: Nekhbet
description: Nekhbet, the ancient Egyptian goddess, was the patron deity of the city of Nekheb (modern El Kab) and, consequently, of Upper Egypt. She is typically depicted as a white Egyptian vulture, instantly recognizable by its distinctive plumage and bald head. Nekhbet’s association with royalty is profound, often appearing alongside Wadjet, the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt, as protectors of the pharaoh. This duality symbolized the unification of the two lands under a single ruler. Her role extended beyond mere protection; she embodied divine motherhood and nurturing, safeguarding the pharaoh from harm and ensuring his legitimacy. In art, she is often shown hovering above the pharaoh, wings outstretched in a gesture of protection, sometimes holding the shen ring, a symbol of eternity and divine power. Nekhbet's enduring presence throughout Egyptian history highlights her significance as a powerful symbol of kingship, protection, and the enduring strength of the Egyptian state.
subtitle: Protectress of Upper Egypt

keyMyths.title: Nekhbet's Protection of the Pharaoh
keyMyths.description: Nekhbet was believed to protect the pharaoh in battle and in life. She was often depicted hovering over the pharaoh, her wings spread wide to shield him from harm. This imagery reinforced the idea of the pharaoh's divine right to rule and his connection to the gods. The vulture imagery, while sometimes misinterpreted, was a symbol of maternal protection, as vultures were believed to fiercely protect their young. Nekhbet's presence ensured the pharaoh's safety and the stability of the kingdom.
keyMyths.source: Wilkinson, Richard H. *The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt*.

keyMyths.title: Nekhbet and the Unification of Egypt
keyMyths.description: Nekhbet, as the goddess of Upper Egypt, played a crucial role in the mythology surrounding the unification of Egypt. Alongside Wadjet, the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt, she represented the two lands united under a single crown. Their presence together symbolized the harmony and balance achieved through the unification, with the pharaoh acting as the unifying force. This duality ensured peace and prosperity for the entire kingdom, solidifying the pharaoh's authority over both regions. The two goddesses were often depicted together on royal regalia.
keyMyths.source: Hart, George. *A Dictionary of Egyptian Gods and Goddesses*.

keyMyths.title: Nekhbet as a Divine Mother
keyMyths.description: Beyond her role as a protector of kingship, Nekhbet was also venerated as a divine mother figure. She was associated with childbirth and nurturing, offering protection to expectant mothers and newborn children. Her maternal qualities were reflected in her depiction as a vulture, a bird known for its protective instincts towards its young. This aspect of Nekhbet's character made her a popular deity among women, who sought her blessings for safe pregnancies and healthy children. She embodied the nurturing and protective aspects of motherhood within the divine realm.
keyMyths.source: Teeter, Emily. *Religion and Ritual in Ancient Egypt*.
